coredata.dc:title: Sensitive electrochemiluminescence aptasensor for chlorpyrifos detection based on resonance energy transfer between MoS2/CdS nanospheres and Ag/CQDs
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Jianhui
Chen, Peipei
Xia, Fangquan
Liu, Zhen
Liu, Hanbiao
Yi, Jiangle
Zhou, Changli
coredata.dc:description: A "signal on" aptasensor was fabricated for chlorpyrifos detection by a highly efficient electrochemiluminescence resonance energy transfer (ECL-RET) strategy between MoS2/CdS nanospheres and silver/carbon quantum dots (Ag/CQDs). Compared with the conventional MoS2/CdS nanosheets, the MoS2/CdS nanospheres have a larger specific surface area and electron density, which makes the catalytic effect on K2S2O8 stronger and has higher luminescence intensity. As a receptor, through the synergistic effect of Ag NPs and CQDs, it has a very wide absorption band between 300 and 600 nm. The absorption band and the emission spectrum of MoS2/CdS completely overlap, and the high quantum yield of Ag/CQDs can achieve efficient energy resonance transfer. The proposed ECL aptasensor provided a linear range from 10 nM to 1.0 fM for chlorpyrifos detection and the detection limit of 0.35 fM (S/N = 3) under optimal experimental conditions. The method was successfully applied to the detection of chlorpyrifos in fruit and vegetable samples with satisfactory results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: ECL-RET
Aptasensor
MoS2/CdS nanosphere
Silver/carbon quantum dots
Chlorpyrifos

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of the aptasensor and measurement procedure
ECL behavior of different modified electrodes
Detection of chlorpyrifos with the proposed aptasensor
Reproducibility, selectivity, and stability of chlorpyrifos aptasensor
Application of the ECL aptasensor in real samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China
Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images (A-C) and SEM (a-c) of MoS2/CdS nanospheres, Au NPs@MoS2/CdS and Ag/CQDs.

XRD spectra of (A) CdS nanospheres, MoS2/CdS nanospheres and (B) Ag/CQDs. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy of Cd 3d (C), Mo 3d (D), S 2p (E) and Au 4f (F) in Au NPs@MoS2/CdS nanospheres. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy of Ag 3d (G), C 1s (H) and O 1s (I) in Ag/CQDs.
ECL-potential curves of different electrodes. a-e: GCE, MoS2, CdS, MoS2/CdS nanosheets, MoS2/CdS nanospheres, Insert: Electron transfer process of MoS2/CdS composites.

(A) wavelength-intensity diagram of Au NPs@MoS2/CdS composite, UV absorption spectrum of CQDs, Ag NPs, Ag/CQDs. (B) The ECL intensity-time curves of Au NPs@MoS2/CdS (a), Au NPs@MoS2/CdS and CQDs (b), Au NPs@MoS2/CdS and Ag NPs (c), Au NPs@MoS2/CdS and Ag/CQDs (d).
CV (A), EIS (B) and ECL (C) curves of different electrodes. Insert: equivalent circuit diagram.
(A) ECL cuvers of different concentrations of chlorpyrifos, (B) the corresponding linear calibration curve. a-h:10−15, 10-14, 10-13, 10-12, 10-11, 10-10, 10-9, 10-8 M. Error bar = RSD (n = 3).
The illustration of modified aptasensor and response mechanism.
Performance comparison between this sensor and other methods.
Determination of Chlorpyrifos in vegetables and fruits (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Sensitive electrochemiluminescence aptasensor for chlorpyrifos detection based on resonance energy transfer between MoS2/CdS nanospheres and Ag/CQDs

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Interfacial Reaction & Sensing Analysis in Universities of Shandong, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan 250022, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Interfacial Reaction & Sensing Analysis in Universities of Shandong
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Interfacial Reaction & Sensing Analysis in Universities of Shandong, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan 250022, P.R.China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A "signal on" aptasensor was fabricated for chlorpyrifos detection by a highly efficient electrochemiluminescence resonance energy transfer (ECL-RET) strategy between MoS2/CdS nanospheres and silver/carbon quantum dots (Ag/CQDs). Compared with the conventional MoS2/CdS nanosheets, the MoS2/CdS nanospheres have a larger specific surface area and electron density, which makes the catalytic effect on K2S2O8 stronger and has higher luminescence intensity. As a receptor, through the synergistic effect of Ag NPs and CQDs, it has a very wide absorption band between 300 and 600 nm. The absorption band and the emission spectrum of MoS2/CdS completely overlap, and the high quantum yield of Ag/CQDs can achieve efficient energy resonance transfer. The proposed ECL aptasensor provided a linear range from 10 nM to 1.0 fM for chlorpyrifos detection and the detection limit of 0.35 fM (S/N = 3) under optimal experimental conditions. The method was successfully applied to the detection of chlorpyrifos in fruit and vegetable samples with satisfactory results.

ce:section.ce:para: Organophosphorus pesticides are widely used in agricultural production. It also protects plants from pests and diseases while also bringing food safety problems [1]. Chlorpyrifos is increasingly becoming a widely used organophosphorus insecticide due to its broad spectrum. It can effectively hold-up acetylcholine esterase and blocking-up signal transmission between neurocyte. Therefore, it will lead to the central nervous system failure of the pest, and ultimately kill the pest [2]. Although chlorpyrifos has a very good result on the control of pests and diseases, its latent poisonousness and contaminate are also very serious [3–5]. Official methods for chlorpyrifos detection usually involve sophisticated instrumentation like gas chromatography (GC), high-pressure liquid chromatography (HPLC), and mass spectrometry (MS) [6–9]. However, these complex, expensive and time-consuming testing methods have encouraged our need for a fast, simple, in situ detection of chlorpyrifos [10].

Electrochemiluminescence (ECL) is a chemiluminescence phenomenon triggered by electrochemical methods. It is a product of chemiluminescence and electrochemical combination and is a comprehensive analytical technique [11–13]. Recently, sensors based on resonance energy transfer strategies have attracted the attention of more and more researchers [14,15]. Energy transfer is the transfer of electron-excited donor chromophore energy to a receptor through a non-radiative dipole [16,17]. Electrochemiluminescence-resonance energy transfer (ECL-RET) combines the advantages of both and is a new field with great potential for development. It does not require excitation of the light source, has less background noise, and avoids the influence of scattered light, and has been widely applied to the field of biosensors [18–22]. Zhu's research team reported ECL-RET between Luminox as a donor and CdSe/ZnS QDs as an acceptor under neutral conditions [23]. In 2017, the ECL-RET system including Luminos (donor) and silicon quantum dots (acceptor) was established for sensitive detection of lysozyme [24]. Nie and his research members studied the ECL-RET system between Luminol as a donor graphene quantum dot as an acceptor for the determination of H2O2 [25]. Compared with these organic dyes as emitters, ECL nanoparticle donors are an ideal choice due to their excellent properties (such as high ECL efficiency, low cost, and easy modification). Among these nanoparticle donors, CdS has high stability, simple preparation, and unique luminescence characteristics, and MoS2 has attracted wide attention due to its excellent thermal stability and narrow band gap [26–28]. Some reports have found that CdS and MoS2 can form composites with ideal heterojunction structures [29–31], which greatly improves the ECL strength. Although MoS2/CdS complexes have been used in ECL sensors, most of these sensors were singal-off sensors [32]. This signal-off mode severely hinders the gain of these sensors. In contrast, signal-on sensors are able to obtain much ameliorative signaling because the background current detected without target is reduced [33]. At the same time, MoS2/CdS composites used in ECL sensors were mostly sheet-like structures. The size of MoS2/CdS composite nanoplatelets is uncontrollable [34], and the ECL performance is low, which is not suitable for the determination of trace pesticide residues in complex matrices.

The good overlap between donor and acceptor is the basic condition of ECL-RET, so the choice of acceptor is crucial. Quantum dots have a wider excitation spectrum and a narrower emission band, which makes them good candidates for energy receptors in ECL-RET biosensors [35]. As an emerging ECL emitter, CQDs have several distinct advantages over QDs, including excellent chemical inertness and ease of labeling [36]. However, due to the narrow absorption band of CQDs between 300 and 400 nm [37,38], they are rarely used as acceptors in ECL sensors. Therefore, it is of great significance to widen the absorption band of CQDs and expand its application in ECL-RET.

In this paper, spherical MoS2/CdS with controlled size and morphology and good ECL luminescence performance was prepared. Compared with the conventional MoS2/CdS nanosheets, the MoS2/CdS nanospheres have a larger specific surface area and electron density, which makes the catalytic effect on K2S2O8 stronger and has higher luminescence intensity. At the same time, Ag/CQDs complexes were prepared as receptors and used to label the aptamers. Through the synergistic effect of Ag NPs and CQDs, it has a very wide absorption band between 300 and 600 nm. The absorption band and the emission spectrum of MoS2/CdS completely overlap, and the high quantum yield of Ag/CQDs can achieve efficient energy resonance transfer. Then, a "signal on" aptasensor was constructed based on a highly efficient ECL-RET strategy between MoS2/CdS nanospheres and Ag/CQDs. The complementary strand (cDNA) of the chlorpyrifos aptamer was modified Au NPs@MoS2/CdS/GCE and then combined with Ag/CQDs-aptamer by the principle of base-complement pairing. Efficient energy resonance transfer between MoS2/CdS and Ag/CQDs decreased ECL emissions. Chlorpyrifos could destroy the double helix structure, caused Ag/CQDs-aptamer left from the electrode surface. The ECL intensity of MoS2/CdS was also recovered (signal-on). This ECL aptasensor based on energy resonance transfer of MoS2/CdS nanospheres and Ag/CQDs enabled highly sensitive detection of chlorpyrifos. The proposed ECL sensor has good analytical performance in fruit and vegetable sample detection.

L-cysteine, ammonium molybdate tetrahydrate, cadmium acetate dehydrate and ammonium bicarbonate were purchased from shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. Sodium citrate dehydrate and sodium borohydride were purchased from Aladdin. Chlorpyrifos, acetamiprid, carbendazim, methyl parathion, cypermethrin, deltamethrin were bought from Shanghai Pesticide Research Institute Co., Ltd. All the oligonucleotides were synthesized and purified by Sangon Biological Engineering Technology and Services Co.,Ltd. (Shanghai, China) and the sequences were listed as follows:
Chlorpyrifos aptamer (aptamer):
5′-NH2-(CH2)6−CCTGCCACGCTCCGCAAGCTTAGGGTTACGCCTGCAGCGATTCTTGATCGCGCTGCTGGTAATCCTTCTTTAAGCTTGGCACCCGCATCGT-3'
Complementary strand of chlorpyrifos aptamer (cDNA):
5′-NH2-(CH2)6-CGGGTGCCAAGCTTA-3'

The ECL emission was monitored with a model MPI-A electrocheminescence analyzer (Xi’an Remax, China). Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was monitored with an electrochemical workstation (CHI 760E, Shanghai Chenhua Instrument Co.). Scanning electron microscope (SEM) images were recorded with a Quanta FEG250 field emission environmental SEM (FEI, United States) operated at 4 KV. Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was tested on a Philips PW1710 instrument (Cu K a radiation, 1.5418 Å). This experiment was performed with a conventional three-electrode system, in which the modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was the working electrode, a platinum wire was the counter electrode and an Ag/AgCl (Sat.KCl) was the reference electrode.

Preparation of MoS2/CdS nanospheres: The preparation of MoS2/CdS nanospheres was slightly modified based on previous reports [39]. 0.2156 g of sodium citrate·dihydrate and 0.0848 g of l-cysteine were dissolved in 35 mL of ethylenediamine, and the mixture was stirred with a magnetic stirrer for half an hour to obtain a clear solution, then 0.0128 g ammonium molybdate·tetrahydrate and 0.128 g cadmium acetate dehydrate were added to the mixed liquor and kept on stirring for 2 h. Thereafter, it was transferred to autoclave and reacted in a vacuum furnace for eight hours at 150℃. After the reaction, the compound was washed several times with anhydrous alcohol and ultra-pure water, after that the washing products were dispersed in 5 mL ultra-pure water for storage and use.
Preparation of Au NPs: Sodium borohydride ice 0.1 M of 1.2 mL was injected into 40 mL aqueous solution containing 0.25 mM sodium citrate dihydrate and HAuCl4(1% wt), stirred and aged for 6 h.

Preparation of Au NPs@MoS2/CdS composites: 260 μL of MoS2/CdS nanosphere solution and 6 mL of Au NPs solution were mixed and stirred overnight. Then, the materials were centrifuged with absolute ethanol and ultra-pure water for 5 min at a speed of 6000 rpm, and then the washed products were dispersed in ultra-pure water for storage and use.

At first, sodium citrate·dihydrate (0.8 g) and ammonium hydrogencarbonate (6.0 g) were dissolved in 40 mL ultrapure water. It was stirred to dissolve completely and transfered to autoclave for four hours at 180℃. The product obtained above was dialyzed against a dialysis membrane to obtain CQDs had a uniform particle size. Next, 0.06 g AgNO3 was added to 7 mL CQDs to mix evenly. Then, 20 mg sodium borohydride was weighed and stirred in the above-solution for half an hour and washed with ultrapure water to remove unreacted reagents. Finally, 2.0 mL of Ag/CQDs mixture was mixed with 500.0 μL of the chlorpyrifos aptamer strand and incubated overnight in a refrigerator. The solution of the hatched Ag/CQDs-aptamer composite was centrifuged and then dispersed in 3.0 mL of ultrapure water for use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the aptasensor and measurement procedure
ce:section.ce:para: The fabrication of the aptasensor mentioned above adopts the method of layer-by-layer assembly. First, GCE was polished and washed. 8 μL of Au NPs@MoS2/CdS dispersion (1 mg/mL) was distributed on the treated GCE surface and dried naturally to obtain Au NPs@MoS2/CdS/GCE. 8 μL of the cDNA (1.0 μM) was dropped to the Au NPs@MoS2/CdS/GCE surface, the modified electrode was cleaned by PBS pH 7.4 after overnight incubation at 4℃. Then, 4 μL of 10 μM 6-mercaptohexanol (MCH) was added to block the non-specific binding site. Finally, 8 μL of Ag/CQDs-aptamer was applied to the surface of the modified electrode, incubated at 37℃ for an hour, rinsed with PBS buffer of pH 7.4 for storage at 4℃. The fabrication process of the aptasensor was shown in Scheme 1 .

Determination of chlorpyrifos: Different concentrations of chlorpyrifos were dropped to the surface of the constructed above aptasensor, incubated at 37℃ for an hour, then washed with PBS buffer pH 7.4. The aptasensor was tested in PBS of pH 7.4 containing 0.1 M K2S2O8 with MPI-A electrocheminescence analyzer in the potential range of -2.0∼0 V (photomultiplier tube 800 V) and recorded the intensity of light.

The morphology and size of MoS2/CdS composites, Au NPs@MoS2/CdS and Ag/CQDs were measured by TEM and SEM. As shown in Fig.1 , the CdS/MoS2 composite material has a spherical network structure with uniform size (Fig.1A and a). A large number of Au NPs were deposited on the surface of MoS2/CdS nanospheres (Fig.1B and b). In the TEM and SEM image of Ag/CQDs, the hyper branched structures were observed (Fig.1C and c). The energy dispersive X-ray (EDS) of the elemental composition of the MoS2/CdS nanospheres, Au NPs@MoS2/CdS and Ag/CQDs were depicted in Fig.S1. The results showed that the prepared MoS2/CdS nanospheres consist of Mo, S, Cd, C, O, and the Au element was present in Au NPs@MoS2/CdS. The presence of Ag, C, and O was observed in the Ag/CQDs complex. The above results indicated that MoS2/CdS nanospheres、Au NPs@MoS2/CdS and Ag/CQDs were successfully fabricated

The XRD spectra of CdS and MoS2/CdS nanospheres were shown in Fig.2 A. The diffraction peaks at 24.9°, 27.1°, 28.3°, 43.9°, 48.1°, and 52.3° are assigned to the (100), (002), (101), (110), (103) and (112) crystal planes of CdS, respectively. For the MoS2/CdS composites, the characteristic diffraction peaks of CdS did not change significantly. In addition, the characteristic peaks of MoS2 appeared at 14.5° and 37.4°, respectively, which belonged to the (002) and (101) crystal planes of MoS2 [29]. As shown in Fig.2B, five distinct diffraction peaks of Ag/CQDs were observed, wherein the characteristic peak at 20.7° corresponds to the (002) crystal plane of the CQDs. At the same time, the diffraction peaks at 39°, 46.1°, 66°, and 79.5° are consistent with the standard spectrum of silver nanowires (JCPDS card 04-0783), corresponding to (111), (200), (220) and (311), respectively. The high-resolution spectrum of Cd 3d (Fig.2C) showed two peaks centered at 414.4 and 404.7 eV, which are in good agreement with the peaks of Cd 3d3/2 and Cd 3d5/2, and a strong peak for Mo 3d at 228 eV (Fig.2D). For the XPS signal of S 2p, the two fitted peaks at 162 and 160.8 eV are corresponding to S 2p1/2 and S 2p3/2 in the form of S 2p (Fig.2E), respectively. The two centered peaks at 83.8 and 87.9 eV are correspond to Au 4f7/2 and Au 4f5/2 (Fig.2F), respectively [40,41]. The above XPS analysis further revealed that Au NPs@MoS2/CdS nanospheres with heterostructures were obtained. As shown in Fig.2G-I, Ag 3d, C 1s, and O 1s peaks appeared in the XPS spectrum of Ag/CQDs. The XRD and XPS results further confirmed the successful preparation of Au NPs@MoS2/CdS nanospheres and Ag/CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ECL behavior of different modified electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: Fig.3 showed the ECL-potential curves of different modified electrodes with a potential range of -2.0 to 0.0 V in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.4) containing 0.1 M K2S2O8. The ECL response of the bare electrode was very low (curve a). For pure MoS2 and CdS, the ECL response was significantly increased by about 3900 a.u. (curve b) and 7990 a.u. (curve c), respectively. When CdS doped with MoS2, the ECL intensity was approximately doubled (curve d). This is because CdS is an n-type semiconductor. When electrons are injected into the interface region and the applied potential is lower than the flat band potential, the energy band will bend downward. Then, the potential continues to drop to a more negative value, the Fermi level will merge into the conduction band, forming a degenerate state of the interface, and the semiconductor will be converted into a conductor such as a metal. The MoS2 component acts as a co-catalyst, which helps transfer electrons from the conduction band of CdS to persulfate anion radicals, thereby accelerating the transfer of charge carriers and causing a significant increase in luminous intensity (Insert). We further studied the ECL properties of MoS2/CdS composites with different morphologies at the same conditions. Since the MoS2/CdS nanospheres (curve e) have a larger specific surface area and electron density than the MoS2/CdS nanosheets (curve d), the catalytic effect on K2S2O8 is stronger to make the MoS2/CdS nanospheres have greater luminescence intensity.

In order to prove the energy resonance transfer between Ag/CQDs and Au NPs@MoS2/CdS, the emission spectra of Au NPs@ MoS2/CdS and the absorption spectra of CQDs and Ag/CQDs were determined. As shown in Fig.4 A, the maximum emission wavelength of Au NPs@MoS2/CdS was 500 nm. CQDs had a distinct ultraviolet absorption peak only at a wavelength of 300∼400 nm and absorption peak of a typical Ag NPs at 430 nm [42]. Due to the synergistic effect of CQDs and Ag NPs, Ag/CQD had a wide and strong absorption band of 300∼600 nm [37,38]. The absorption range is larger and completely overlaps with the emission spectrum of Au NPs@MoS2/CdS. Immediately, the ECL experiment further confirmed the ECL-RET process. As shown in Fig.4B, the light intensity of Au NPs@MoS2/CdS was large (curve a), and the light intensities were decreased to different degrees after loading CQDs (curve b) and Ag NPs (curve c) to Au NPs@MoS2/CdS, respectively. However, the light intensity dropped significantly after loading Ag/CQDs composite on Au NPs@MoS2/CdS (curve d). It was shown that efficient RET occurred between Au NPs@MoS2/CdS and Ag/CQDs.

Fig.5 A showed CV curves (scanning rate of 0.1 V/s) in 0.1 M KCl containing 1 mM [Fe(CN)6]3− under different assembly steps. Compared with bare GCE (curve a), the peak current of Au NPs@MoS2/CdS/GCE (curve b) was significantly reduced because the macromolecular complex Au NPs@MoS2/CdS hinders electron transfer. Furthermore, as the non-conductive cDNA (curve c), MCH (curve d) and Ag/CQDs-aptamer (curve e) were modified to the electrode surface, the peak current continued to decrease. As a result, with the addition of 1 nM chlorpyrifos, some Ag/CQDs-aptamer dropped from the electrode surface and the peak current increased (curve f). EIS can characterize the interface properties of different modified electrodes. This semi-circular part represents the charge transfer process and represents the interfacial resistance value (Rct). Fig.5B showed the EIS behaviors in 0.1 M KCl containing 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]4-/3- at different assembly steps. The Rct of bare GCE was 93.6 Ω (curve a). When Au NPs@MoS2/CdS (curve b) was modified to the surface of the bare GCE, the Rct increased to 351.2 Ω due to an increase in interface resistance. Moreover, when the cDNA (curve c) and MCH (curve d) were continuous incubation on electrodes, Rct increased gradually (516.8 and 634 Ω respectively), which owing to hinder electron transfer from solution to electrode surface. After Ag/CQDs-aptamer (curve e) was modified on MCH/cDNA/Au NPs@MoS2/CdS/GCE, the Rct increased significantly to 1130.4 Ω because larger size Ag/CQDs-aptamer modifications would hinder electron transfer. When 1 nM of chlorpyrifos was added, some Ag/CQDs-aptamer fell off the electrode surface, and the Rct decreased to 824.2 Ω (curve f). As shown in Fig.5C, the GCE had no ECL response due to the absence of illuminants. However, after the GCE surface was loaded with the Au NPs@MoS2/CdS (curve b), there was a strong ECL signal. After the layer was modified with cDNA (curve c) and MCH (curve d), the ECL signal was slightly attenuated because the biomolecule was not conductive and blocks electron transfer on the electrode surface. It was worth noting that when Ag/CQDs-aptamer was modified to the surface of the electrode, there was a sudden decrease in the intensity spectrum of ECL owing to the ECL-RET between Ag/CQDs and MoS2/CdS nanospheres (curve e). With the addition of 1 nM chlorpyrifos, part of Ag/CQDs-aptamer fell off the electrode surface, and the ECL light intensity was partially increased (curve f). The characterization of ECL was fully consistent with the results of electrochemical impedance.
a-f：GCE, Au NPs@MoS2/CdS/GCE, cDNA/Au NPs@MoS2/CdS/GCE, MCH/cDNA/Au NPs@MoS2/CdS/GCE, Ag/CQDs-aptamer/MCH/cDNA/Au NPs@MoS2/CdS/GCE, Chlorpyrifos/Ag/CQDs-aptamer/MCH/cDNA/Au NPs@MoS2/CdS/GCE.

In order to improve the sensitivity of aptasensor for chlorpyrifos (1 nM) detection, the incubation time, potassium persulfate concentration and pH condition of PBS solution were optimized. When the incubation time was 60 min, the ECL intensity reached the maximum. When incubation-time was more than 60 min, the change in light intensity was very small and almost remained stable (Fig. S2 A). As the concentration of S2O8 2− increased, the ECL intensity gradually increased. The strength of ECL tended to be stable when the concentration of S2O8 2− was higher than 0.10 M (Fig.S2 B). The ECL intensity increased first and then decreased with the pH value of PBS and peaks at pH 7.4 (Fig.S2 C). In summary, incubation time 60 min, 0.10 M S2O8 2− and pH 7.4 were selected as the best experimental conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of chlorpyrifos with the proposed aptasensor
Reproducibility, selectivity, and stability of chlorpyrifos aptasensor
ce:section.ce:para: For verified the reproducibility of the aptasensor, we performed a parallel experiment on seven modified electrodes with a chlorpyrifos concentration of 1.0 nM, as shown in Fig.S3 A. The results of seven parallel experiments were recorded and the relative standard deviation (RSD) was calculated to be 4.07 %, which indicated the sensor had good reproducibility. For studied the selectivity of the prepared aptasensor, the interference experiments were tested. ECL strength of methyl parathion, carbendazim, cypermethrin, and acetamiprid did not change significantly compared with that of the blank. However, the ECL intensity increased when 1.0 nM chlorpyrifos coexisted with the above several pesticides (10.0 nM), and the ECL intensity of the mixture approximated that of chlorpyrifos alone, which indicated that this aptasensor had good specificity (Fig. S3 B). In addition, the prepared aptasensor was placed at 4 ℃ for 16 days, and the ECL intensity was 95 % of the initial light intensity, indicated that this aptasensor had acceptable stability (Fig. S3 C).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the ECL aptasensor in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The vegetables and fruits used in the experiment were purchased at local supermarkets. First, 100 g of each sample accurately was weighed, crushed it with a juicer, and dispersed into 100 mL of 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.4). After sonication for 5 min, the suspension was centrifuged (5 min, 8000 rpm), and the supernatant was taken for subsequent measurement. We compared the results of the sensor with high performance liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry used the same sample. As shown in Table 2 , the results obtained by the developed ECL sensor were consistent with those obtained by HPLC-MS. The content of chlorpyrifos was less than 50 ng/g of chlorpyrifos residue limit. The recovery range was between 96 % and 104 % and RSD was less than 5.0 %, which was considered to be a reference method for the detection of chlorpyrifos poisoning in actual samples.

In this paper, a "signal-on" aptasensor was constructed for chlorpyrifos detection based on a highly efficient ECL-RET strategy between MoS2/CdS nanospheres and Ag/CQDs. On the one hand, the MoS2/CdS nanospheres have a larger specific surface area and electron density, which makes the catalytic effect on K2S2O8 stronger and has higher luminescence intensity. On the other hand, Ag/CQDs as receptors has a broad and strong absorption band, which can absorb the light emitted by MoS2/CdS nanospheres well to achieve ECL-RET. Based on the above advantages, the constructed aptasensor possesses the characteristics of high specificity and sensitivity, which provides a new idea for the detection of chlorpyrifos in agricultural production and environmental samples.

Jianhui Liu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - original draft. Peipei Chen: Validation, Writing - review & editing. Fangquan Xia: Resources, Data curation, Supervision. Zhen Liu: Formal analysis, Writing - review & editing. Hanbiao Liu: Visualization. Jiangle Yi: Visualization. Changli Zhou: Resources, Data curation, Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Foundation of Shandong Provincial Natural Science Foundation, China (ZR2017MB063), and National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC) (No. 31370090).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2020.128098.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Mechanisms of synergistic neurotoxicity induced by two high risk pesticide residues-Chlorpyrifos and Carbofuran via oxidative stress
Toxicol. In Vitro

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. J. Fu, P. Li, J. Song, S. Y. Zhang, H. Z. Xie, Mechanisms of synergistic neurotoxicity induced by two high risk pesticide residues-Chlorpyrifos and Carbofuran via oxidative stress, Toxicol. In Vitro 54 (2019) 338-344.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Development of molecularly imprinted polymers-surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy/colorimetric dual sensor for determination of chlorpyrifos in apple juice
Sens. Actuators, B

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Feng, Y. Hu, L. Ma, X. Lu, Development of molecularly imprinted polymers-surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy/colorimetric dual sensor for determination of chlorpyrifos in apple juice, Sens. Actuators, B 241 (2017) 750-757.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Jianhui Liu is a current master student, studies in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of Jinan. He is working on constructing biological sensor.
Peipei Chen is a current master student, studies in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of Jinan. She is working on constructing biological sensor.
Fangquan Xia received his Ph.D. degree from Shandong University in 2006. His research works mainly focus on electrochemistry.

Zhen Liu received his Ph.D. degree from Shandong University in 2016. He is the postdoctor in the University of Jinan. His research interests focus on the design and synthesis of plasmonic nanomaterials and their applications in the photoelectrocatalysis, SERS and sensor field.
Hanbiao Liu is a current master student, studies in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of Jinan. He is working on constructing biological sensor.
Jiangle Yi is a current master student, studies in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of Jinan. He is working on constructing biological sensor.

Changli Zhou is a professor of analytical chemistry in University of Jinan. He received his Ph.D. degree in analytical chemistry from Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, PR China in 1999. His research interests are focused on electroanalytical chemistry and biosensors.
